$comment: Scoring weights for AI-readiness audit v1.4. Weights are normalized to sum to 1.00. Organised into three tiers based on observed impact on AI agent operability from the MUI v1.3 audit. v1.4 introduces formalised sub-checks and a documented aggregation formula. v1.3 and v1.4 scores are not directly comparable.

scoring_methodology.$comment: New in v1.4. Documents the two-layer scoring system. v1.3 used implicit aggregation. v1.4 formalises sub-checks and the aggregation formula. Scores from v1.3 and v1.4 are not directly comparable.
scoring_methodology.dimension_score_formula: average of sub-check scores x 25, rounded to nearest integer, producing 0-100
scoring_methodology.overall_score_formula: sum of (dimension_score x weight) across all dimensions
scoring_methodology.override_rule: If any sub-check within a dimension scores 0, dimension severity is forced to blocker regardless of composite score

severity_thresholds.$comment: Default severity boundaries applied per dimension. Client scoring configs may override individual dimensions with stricter or more lenient thresholds.

phase_readiness_thresholds.$comment: Phase readiness logic. The blocker gate does the heavy lifting: a system with any dimension-level blocker is not ready regardless of overall score.

conditional_pass.$comment: Also applies when overall < 50 but zero blockers: warnings present but no structural failures

token_architecture_depth.$comment: Tier 1: agent cannot operate. Highest weight. Without three-layer architecture, an agent cannot map semantic intent to component properties — it must guess which token applies where. MUI scored 30 here and it was the top blocker. v1.3 weight was 0.12; raised to 0.14 because the MUI audit proved this dimension determined the verdict more than token implementation did.

component_to_token_binding.$comment: Tier 1: agent cannot operate. Tied highest weight. Unbound component properties force the agent to infer token relationships — a primary source of hallucination. MUI scored 25 and it was the second blocker. v1.3 weight was 0.12; raised to 0.14 for the same reason as token_architecture_depth.

component_description_coverage.$comment: Tier 1: agent cannot operate. Without descriptions carrying functional intent, an agent cannot decide which component fits a use case. MUI scored 15 — 64.2% had no description, 96.2% of existing descriptions were code snippets. v1.3 weight was 0.07; raised significantly because the MUI audit showed this dimension was a blocker, not a nice-to-have.

documentation_quality.$comment: Tier 1: agent cannot operate. Distinct from coverage (Dimension 6): this scores whether documentation is structured, concise, and useful to an agent — not just whether it exists. MUI scored 10 — the lowest of all dimensions. v1.3 weight was 0.06 (lowest); doubled because the MUI audit proved documentation quality was a blocker equal to coverage.

token_implementation.$comment: Tier 2: agent operates, output quality degrades. Tokens are the foundation, but MUI scored 72 here and was still not ready. The presence of tokens is necessary but not sufficient — architecture and binding matter more. v1.3 weight was 0.15 (highest); reduced because the MUI evidence showed high token implementation did not prevent a not-ready verdict.

alias_chain_integrity.$comment: Tier 2: agent operates, output quality degrades. Broken chains produce wrong values, but MUI scored 55 and the alias issues were warnings, not blockers. The real failures were architectural. v1.3 weight was 0.15 (tied highest); reduced because the MUI audit showed this was a quality issue, not a blocking one.

accessibility_intent_coverage.$comment: Tier 2: agent operates, output quality degrades. New in v1.4. Accessibility signals (focus states, touch targets, contrast, keyboard navigation) determine whether generated code meets WCAG requirements. Without them, an agent produces visually correct but inaccessible output. Weighted equal to alias chain integrity — both degrade output quality without blocking operation entirely.

platform_readiness_gap.$comment: Tier 2: agent operates, output quality degrades. Renamed from web_readiness_gap in v1.4 per decision 004. Platform-specific metadata (ARIA roles for web, accessibility labels for mobile) determines whether generated code is production-ready for the target platform. v1.3 weight was 0.08; slightly reduced as some concerns moved to the new accessibility dimension.

primitive_naming.$comment: Tier 3: system hygiene. Machine-parseable naming improves automated mapping but an agent can compensate if bindings and aliases are intact. v1.3 weight was 0.10; reduced because the MUI audit showed naming issues were notes, not blockers or warnings that affected the verdict.

naming_convention_consistency.$comment: Tier 3: system hygiene. Inconsistent naming degrades pattern matching but does not block agent use. v1.3 weight was 0.08; reduced for the same reason as primitive naming.

governance.$comment: Tier 3: system hygiene. Governance rules improve long-term maintainability and drift detection but their absence does not impair a single audit or code generation run. v1.3 weight was 0.07; reduced as the lowest-impact dimension for agent operability.

token_implementation.description: Typography tokens as Variables, not duplicated as text styles
Elevation/effect tokens as Variables, not legacy styles only

alias_chain_integrity.description: Local alias chains resolve without breaks
Remote alias references documented and resolvable
All token categories have semantic-to-primitive alias chains

token_architecture_depth.description: Primitive token layer exists and is populated
Semantic token layer exists and aliases to primitives
Component-level token layer exists and aliases to semantic
Non-colour categories (spacing, typography, elevation) have layered architecture

primitive_naming.description: Token names use full words and slash separators
Naming convention consistent within each collection
Names include namespace or category prefix

component_to_token_binding.description: Component-level token layer provides explicit binding documentation

component_description_coverage.description: Percentage of components with any description
Percentage of descriptions carrying functional intent
Description present on component set root entries (not only variants)

naming_convention_consistency.description: Naming convention consistent across token collections
Naming convention consistent across component sets
Names are machine-parseable, no special characters that break parsing

platform_readiness_gap.description: Target platform metadata present in component properties
State variants map to platform interaction states

documentation_quality.description: Documentation captures functional intent (when to use, when not to use)
Documentation useful to an agent (could it make a component selection decision?)

accessibility_intent_coverage.description: Focus states defined as component variants
Touch target sizes meet minimum guidelines
Colour contrast derivable from token alias chain
Keyboard navigation behaviour documented in descriptions
Accessibility considerations mentioned in component descriptions
